steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage states that on average, mammal studies showed larger differences than amphibian studies, but "for every class of animals examined, there were individual studies showing differences well above the average for mammals." That means some individual amphibian studies exceeded the mammal average. The answer must follow logically from this.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — the differences that studies attribute to exposure to anthropogenic noise are likely to be more pronounced for amphibians than they are for mammals.: The passage says the average for mammals was higher, not lower. This contradicts the stated trend. ✗
  • Choice B — some studies of amphibians found larger effects of exposure to anthropogenic noise than some studies of mammals did.: If individual amphibian studies exceeded the mammal average, they necessarily exceeded at least some mammal studies. This follows directly. ✓
  • Choice C — the difference found in the study conducted by Graeme Shannon and colleagues was likely larger than the average difference for studies of black-tailed prairie dogs included in the meta-analysis.: The passage does not compare Shannon's study to the prairie dog average. This is not supported. ✗
  • Choice D — the studies in the meta-analysis that examined mammals were more likely than those that examined amphibians to specify whether the observed effects were detrimental.: The passage says nothing about whether studies specified the nature of effects. ✗
That confirms B is correct.
